topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom was thick with expensive perfume and forced merriment. I leaned against a marble pillar, nursing a glass of champagne that tasted like regret. Then I saw him. He was across the room, laughing easily with a group of people, looking annoyingly polished. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. He looked… happy. A sharp, unpleasant twist coiled in my stomach. I made a deliberate show of scanning the room, ensuring my gaze landed on him, a slow, deliberate sweep that felt like a silent accusation. When our eyes met across the crowded floor, I offered a tight, brittle smile—the kind that doesn't reach the eyes—and then immediately turned my attention back to the canapés, focusing intensely on the tiny cracker in my hand, willing myself to be invisible.

[story 2]

It was a calculated maneuver, really. I’d spent the last hour perfecting my posture, ensuring my dress draped just right, my expression settled into one of serene, detached amusement. When I spotted him near the entrance, a familiar shadow crossing my periphery, a distinct clenching sensation shot up my arm. He caught my eye, and for a split second, I felt a flash of something sharp and unwelcome—a memory suddenly resurfacing with unwelcome clarity. I didn't move immediately. I let the silence stretch, letting the ambient noise of the celebration wash over me while I meticulously adjusted the strap of my clutch. When I finally did approach, the greeting I offered was clipped, overly bright, and utterly devoid of warmth. "Well, look who decided to grace us with their presence," I said, my voice pitched just loud enough for him to hear, carrying a brittle edge that I hoped he would sense.

[story 3]

I had been trying to enjoy the music, to absorb the celebratory atmosphere, but every glance in the general direction of the main entrance felt like a physical pressure building behind my ribs. When I finally spotted him, standing near the buffet table, a wave of intense irritation washed over me. It was a familiar, hot surge, making my palms sweat slightly beneath the silk of my sleeve. I stopped mid-sip of my sparkling wine. Instead of acknowledging him, I deliberately turned my back, focusing entirely on a distant painting on the wall, my shoulders stiffening as if bracing for impact. When he walked closer, I kept my eyes fixed on the canvas, offering nothing but a blank, unreadable expression, a deliberate wall erected between us made entirely of cold indifference.
